elements.sent-2411-001: Transformers have revolutionized Computer Vision (CV) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) through self-attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, due to their complexity, their latent token representations are often difficult to interpret.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce a novel framework that interprets Transformer embeddings, uncovering meaningful semantic patterns within them.
elements.sent-2411-004: Based on this framework, we demonstrate that zero-shot unsupervised semantic segmentation can be performed effectively without any fine-tuning using a model pre-trained for tasks other than segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method reveals the inherent capacity of Transformer models for understanding input semantics and achieves state-of-the-art performance in semantic segmentation, outperforming traditional segmentation models.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, our approach achieves an accuracy of 67.2 \% and an mIoU of 32.9 \% on the COCO-Stuff dataset, as well as an mIoU of 51.9 \% on the PASCAL VOC dataset.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we validate our interpretability framework on LLMs for text summarization, demonstrating its broad applicability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, the Transformer architecture and foundation models, leveraging self-attention mechanisms to capture complex dependencies in text, have transformed Natural Language Processing (NLP) benchmarks \citep{vaswani2017attention, touvron2023llama2openfoundation, albert_q_jiang_mistral_2023, gemmateam2024gemmaopenmodelsbased .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Similarly, Vision Transformers (ViTs) \citep{dosovitskiy2020image have been adapted in Computer Vision (CV) and now serve as the backbone for various tasks such as segmentation and object detection \citep{thisanke2023semantic, liu2021swin .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite their success, understanding the interpretability of Transformers remains a challenge due to the complexity of their latent token representations.

Each leaf node represents a single token relevance map, while higher-level nodes show aggregated clusters based on a clustering threshold $( \zeta)$, which controls the level of detail. Lower $\zeta$ values reveal finer details, while higher values create broader, more general clusters. This approach demonstrates how pre-trained Vision Transformers can perform unsupervised semantic segmentation, identifying meaningful patterns within token representations without requiring additional training or fine-tuning.} \label{fig:hierarchy}
elements.sent-2411-011: Several methods have been developed to enhance the interpretability of CNN-based models \citep{simonyan2014deep, zeiler2014visualizing, selvaraju2017grad .}
elements.sent-2411-012: While some of these can be extended to Transformer architectures, they do not fully leverage the unique attention mechanisms inherent to Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-013: Recent research has introduced interpretability methods specifically designed for Transformers \citep{chefer2021transformer, abnar2020quantifying, vig2019analyzing .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, these approaches primarily focus on explaining final model outputs, providing limited insight into the intermediate processes that lead to predictions.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, \citep{cheninvite maps latent tokens into CLIP's \citep{radford2021learning multi-modal space to find corresponding text descriptions, relying on an external text encoder for interpretability.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, our approach directly interprets the latent space of ViTs, elucidating the role and function of each token within the high-dimensional space without relying on external models.
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper introduces a framework to interpret latent tokens, offering a deeper understanding of the internal workings of Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-018: This understanding enables users to perform image semantic segmentation using pre-trained Transformer-based vision models in an unsupervised, zero-shot manner, without any additional training.
elements.sent-2411-019: We demonstrate that applying semantic segmentation based on our interpretability framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark image segmentation datasets.
elements.sent-2411-020: Drawing inspiration from \citep{chefer2021transformer , our method analyzes the semantic information retained by latent tokens, enabling tasks such as object selection and semantic segmentation without additional training.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We demonstrate that Transformers inherently understand the semantic structure of their input, viewing it as a collection of distinct concepts.
elements.sent-2411-022: Each latent token identifies a specific concept with semantic significance, thereby shedding light on the decision-making process of these models.
elements.sent-2411-023: As shown in Section \ref{sec: Experiment , our framework proves effective in a range of tasks, including semantic segmentation and model interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Most recent unsupervised semantic segmentation methods involve an additional training phase to learn feature representations \citep{sick2024unsupervised, hamilton2022unsupervised, li2023acseg .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our approach, however, utilizes the understanding embedded in pre-trained models to achieve zero-shot segmentation, leveraging their inherent knowledge of images.
elements.sent-2411-026: The stronger a model's comprehension of image content, the more accurately it performs segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-027: We further demonstrate that our method is capable of interpreting large language models (LLMs) at the token level, validating its application in tasks such as text summarization.
elements.sent-2411-028: The main contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-029: We propose a framework for interpreting latent tokens in Transformers, providing valuable insights into model decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-030: By aggregating relevance maps generated by tokens using hierarchical clustering, we achieve zero-shot unsupervised semantic segmentation on pre-trained models, outperforming SOTA methods that require additional training.
elements.sent-2411-031: We demonstrate the capability of our method to interpret LLMs at the token level, showcasing its practical applicability to textual data.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, we demonstrate the interpretation of LLM operations in the text summarization task.
elements.sent-2411-033: This paper is structured as follows: Section \ref{sec_prior reviews interpretability frameworks and previous work on semantic segmentation, highlighting our SOTA results.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In Section \ref{sec: Experiment , we showcase its effectiveness on image and text tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The interpretability of deep learning architectures has become a central focus in AI research \cite{iomll23 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: As models grow in complexity, understanding their decision-making processes is essential for ensuring transparency, reliability, and fairness \cite{tmlt22 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Interpretability not only aids in debugging and performance improvement but also builds trust in AI systems, particularly in fields like healthcare, finance, and autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-040: Opaque models can perpetuate biases and generate unforeseen outcomes, making interpretability crucial for bridging high performance with safe, practical AI deployment \cite{taflq23 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Transformer models, tokens are key to interpreting behavior, with their relationship to spatial locations in images or sequence order adding an important layer to interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-042: This relationship enhances semantic segmentation as a measure for evaluating token-based interpretation frameworks, which this section will explore.
elements.sent-2411-043: Model interpretability is a critical area of research in deep learning, especially for complex models like transformers.
elements.sent-2411-044: Traditional deep learning models, such as CNNs, have been interpreted using various techniques like saliency maps \citep{simonyan2014deep , deconvolutional networks \citep{zeiler2014visualizing , Guided Backpropagation \cite{sfssd14 , Feature Visualization \cite{olah2017feature , Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) \cite{ribeiro2016should , SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) \cite{lundberg2017unified , Class Activation Mapping (CAM) \cite{zhou2016learning , Feature Attribution with Integrated Gradients \cite{sundararajan2017axiomatic , and Grad-CAM \citep{selvaraju2017grad , which highlight the most important regions of the input that influence model predictions.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: While effective for CNNs, these techniques are less suitable for transformer architectures, as they fail to account for the unique self-attention mechanisms that transformers rely on.
elements.sent-2411-046: Anchors is a model-agnostic interpretability method that provides high-precision, locally faithful explanations by generating if-then rules that sufficiently explain a model's prediction for specific inputs \cite{ribeiro2018anchors .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Recent interpretability research on transformers has focused on understanding how these models allocate attention and propagate information.
elements.sent-2411-048: A seminal contribution was made by examining self-attention patterns in transformer-based language models, revealing how attention is distributed across tokens \cite{vig2019analyzing .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Abnar et al. \cite{abnar2020quantifying advanced this by proposing methods to visualize attention flow across layers, aiding in the understanding of information propagation.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Chefer et al. \cite{chefer2021transformer introduced a relevance-based approach, computing relevance scores for each token to provide deeper insights into the model's decision-making process.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, the sensitivity of model predictions to input tokens has been explored as another way to interpret transformer behavior \cite{jain2019attention .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Interpretability methods for reflecting semantic relations within input sequences have been investigated in NLP tasks, aiming to characterize how transformers associate words or tokens across different positions in a sentence for downstream tasks \cite{clark2019does .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Justifying the role of the multi-head attention mechanism in transformers and its contribution to improved performance is another area of study, revealing insights into how transformers operate \cite{ashml19 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Other notable interpretability methods include Interpretability-Aware Visual Transformers (IA-ViT) \cite{qiang2023interpretability , Mechanistic Interpretability \cite{rai2024practical , Concept Transformers \cite{rigotti2021attention , Nested Hierarchical Transformers \cite{zhang2022nested , Tracr \cite{lindner2024tracr , and ViT-net \cite{kim2022vit .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: While many methods focus on explaining model outputs, fewer efforts have been directed toward understanding the intermediate processes within the model.
elements.sent-2411-056: The internal representations, particularly the latent tokens in Vision Transformers (ViTs), remain largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-057: A similar approach is the work by \cite{cheninvite , which focuses on the CLIP model \citep{radford2021learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Their method involves disabling the self-attention mechanism to map the latent tokens into a multimodal space without additional training.
elements.sent-2411-059: However, simply disabling self-attention may introduce distribution shifts, raising concerns about the validity of their results.
elements.sent-2411-060: Furthermore, their approach relies heavily on CLIP's text encoder, which limits its generalizability to other models.
elements.sent-2411-061: Our work overcomes these limitations by focusing on backward-looking analysis, investigating how latent tokens relate to the input rather than looking ahead.
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, we rely solely on one encoder, making our method more applicable to a broader range of models.
elements.sent-2411-063: Unsupervised semantic segmentation has advanced significantly due to self-supervised learning and clustering-based techniques, which reduce reliance on labeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-064: Early approaches, such as Invariant Information Clustering (IIC) \cite{ji2019invariant , employed mutual information to group similar pixels without using labels.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Building on this foundation, PiCIE introduced consistency by integrating photometric and geometric invariances, setting an important precedent for subsequent methods \cite{cho2021picie .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The emergence of Vision Transformers (ViTs) and self-supervised learning marked a major shift in the field.
elements.sent-2411-067: DINO became a pioneering method, extracting rich, meaningful features without the need for labeled data \cite{caron2021emerging .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Its effectiveness in unsupervised segmentation laid the groundwork for more recent advancements.
elements.sent-2411-069: For instance, TransFGU \cite{yin2022transfgu performs semantic segmentation in a top-down manner by deriving class activation maps from DINO models.}
elements.sent-2411-070: STEGO \cite{hamilton2022unsupervised leverages DINO’s features, employing contrastive learning to group similar regions and achieving notable improvements in segmentation accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Subsequent methods further refined these concepts.
elements.sent-2411-072: MaskContrast incorporated clustering techniques to ensure region consistency across different views, enhancing feature representations for unsupervised segmentation \cite{van2021unsupervised .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Leopart utilized self-supervised ViTs to improve pixel grouping, particularly in complex scenes \cite{ziegler2022self .}
elements.sent-2411-074: ACSeg introduced adaptive conceptualization for pixel-level semantic grouping, using an Adaptive Concept Generator (ACG) to dynamically align learnable prototypes with relevant concepts, thereby addressing over- and under-clustering challenges in varied images \cite{li2023acseg .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Leveraging hidden positives for unsupervised semantic segmentation enhances pixel grouping by identifying and utilizing implicit positive relationships within the data, boosting segmentation performance without the need for labeled examples \cite{seong2023leveraging .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Using self-supervised learning and adaptive feature representations to enable models to discover and segment novel, unseen object categories without labeled data have also been investigated \cite{uhlemeyer2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Smooseg introduces a smoothness prior to enhancing unsupervised semantic segmentation by promoting coherent region grouping \cite{lan2023smooseg .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Unsupervised semantic segmentation is also improved by leveraging depth-guided feature correlation and targeted sampling to enhance region consistency and accuracy \cite{sick2024unsupervised .. \cite{gao2022large .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Unlike approaches that depend on self-training, pseudo-labeling, or complex setups, our model ULTra introduces a zero-shot method for unsupervised semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-080: ULTra achieves strong segmentation performance by directly leveraging the semantic information embedded in the latent tokens of pre-trained Transformer models, without the need for additional training or fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-081: Furthermore, ULTra emphasizes explainability within Transformers by illustrating how the model’s latent tokens contribute to segmentation, in contrast to the common practice of using only the CLS token to represent the entire image.
elements.sent-2411-082: This zero-shot, explainability-driven approach offers a novel direction for unsupervised segmentation, demonstrating that Transformer-based architectures can achieve SOTA performance without labeled data or extensive additional processing.

elements.sent-2411-083: In this section, we present our approach for interpreting latent representations in Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-084: We begin with essential preliminaries and notation for clarity, followed by a detailed explanation of our method for interpreting latent tokens and its application to semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-085: The architecture of a typical Transformer can be formulated as follows: the input \( X \) is split into \( n \) tokens \( \{\mathbf{x _i\}_{i=1 ^n \).}}
elements.sent-2411-086: After tokenization, token embeddings \( \{\mathbf{e _i\}_{i=0 ^n \) are computed, where \( \mathbf{e _0 \) corresponds to the CLS token.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Positional encodings \( \text{PE _i \) are added to the \( i \)-th token embedding to incorporate spatial information, resulting in the latent token representation \( \mathbf{z _i^{(1) = \mathbf{e _i + \text{PE _i \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Here, \( \mathbf{z _i^{(l) \) represents a latent token, where \( l \) denotes the layer index with \( l \in \{1, \dots, L\} \) and \( L \) is the total number of layers in the Transformer, and \( i \) represents the \( i \)-th token within the \( l \)-th layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: For each head \( h \in \{1, \dots, H\} \) in the multi-head attention mechanism, the queries, keys, and values corresponding to the \( i \)-th token are obtained via linear transformations, projecting the latent token of dimension \( d \) into dimension \( k \): \begin{equation}Q^{(l)}_h(z_i^{(l-1)}) = (W_{h,q}^{(l)})^T z_i^{(l-1)}, K^{(l)}_h(z_i^{l-1}) = (W_{h,k}^{(l)})^T z^{(l-1)}_i, \nonumber\end{equation} \begin{equation}V^{(l)}_h(z_i^{(l-1)}) = (W_{h,v}^{(l)})^T z_i^{(l-1)},\quad \forall l \in \{2,\dots,L\}\end{equation} where \( W_{h,q ^{(l) , W_{h,k ^{(l) , W_{h,v ^{(l) \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times k \).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The attention weights for each token pair \( (i, j) \) at layer \( l \) and head \( h \) are computed as: \begin{equation}\alpha_{h,i,j}^{(l)} = \text{softmax}_j \left( \frac{\langle Q^{(l)}_h(\mathbf{z}_i^{l-1}), K^{(l)}_h(\mathbf{z}_j^{(l-1)}) \rangle}{\sqrt{k}} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: Then, \( i \)-th token is updated by summing over the weighted values across all heads: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\bar{u}}^{(l)}_i = \sum_{h=1}^H (W_{c,h}^{(l)})^T \sum_{j=1}^n \alpha_{h,i,j}^{(l)} V^{(l)}_h(\mathbf{z}_j^{(l-1)}),\end{equation} where \( W_{c,h \in \mathbb{R ^{k \times d \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The updated token representation \( \mathbf{u _i \) after the attention layer is computed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{u}_i^{(l)} = \text{LayerNorm}(\mathbf{z}_i^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{\bar{u}}^{(l)}_i).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Each token then passes through a feed-forward network: \begin{equation}\mathbf{\bar{z}}_i^{(l)} = (W_2^{(l)})^T ReLU((W_1^{(l)})^T u_i), z_i^{(l)} = LayerNorm(u_i + \mathbf{\bar{z}}_i^{(l)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: A straightforward approach for interpreting a model involves analyzing the semantic flow from the input to the corresponding logits.
elements.sent-2411-096: This can be achieved by adding the attention probability matrix to an identity matrix \( I \), which incorporates skip connections, and then multiplying the result across layers \cite{abnar2020quantifying .}
elements.sent-2411-097: However, a notable challenge arises from multiple attention heads in each Transformer layer.
elements.sent-2411-098: To address this, \cite{chefer2021transformer proposes performing a weighted average across attention heads, with the weights determined by the gradient of the logits with respect to the attention weights.}
elements.sent-2411-099: We employed a modified, simpler version of this method, utilizing only the attention weights rather than the layer’s relevance.
elements.sent-2411-100: In our framework, latent tokens \( \mathbf{z _i^{(l) \) are represented as vectors rather than direct class correspondences, introducing an additional layer of complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: To manage this, an appropriate transformation, such as the energy or euclidean norm of the latent token, is employed as a surrogate.
elements.sent-2411-102: Consequently, we compute the gradient of \( \|\mathbf{z _i^{(l)}\| \) with respect to the attention weights to facilitate the analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In the following equations, we define the relevance map \( S_i^{(l) \in \mathbb{R ^n \), where the \( j \)-th element of this vector represents the importance of the \( j \)-th input token to the targeted latent token \( \mathbf{z _i^{(l) \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Due to the skip connections in the transformer, most of the contribution of \( S_i^{(l) \) is concentrated on \( S_i^{(l) [i-1] \) which makes it hard to analyze other tokens' contribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: To address this issue, we replace this element with the maximum value of other elements, thereby capturing the contributions of additional tokens to the selected token.
elements.sent-2411-107: For vision tasks, we first reshape the relevance map and then upsample it using bilinear or cubic interpolation to match the resolution of the model's input.
elements.sent-2411-108: The resulting higher-dimensional matrix is denoted as \( \Tilde{S \).}
elements.sent-2411-109: This upsampling step is essential for enabling accurate object selection and semantic segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-110: In this section, we aim to examine ULTra's capability to adapt to various tasks involving semantic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-111: Importantly, it requires no additional training, leveraging the inherent understanding within transformers rather than relying on loss functions objective, final layer outputs, or fine-tuning.. Unsupervised Semantic Segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-112: As previously discussed, a relevance map can be defined for each latent token at a fixed layer, with the total number of relevance maps equal to the number of latent tokens.
elements.sent-2411-113: In the context of segmentation, the goal is to assign a class label to each pixel within an image.
elements.sent-2411-114: To achieve this, we employ clustering techniques, such as hierarchical clustering, that do not require a predefined number of classes.
elements.sent-2411-115: These techniques group the relevance maps into \( k \) distinct clusters, where \( k \) is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-116: Ideally, we aim for \( k \) to approximate the actual number of classes present in the image.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our approach provides flexibility in adjusting the value of \( k \) by modifying the cutoff distance threshold $\zeta$ within the clustering algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-118: Increasing $\zeta$ produces fewer, broader clusters that capture general categories, such as background and foreground.
elements.sent-2411-119: Conversely, reducing $\zeta$ allows for finer segmentation, distinguishing more specific features, such as an object's head or hands.
elements.sent-2411-120: To prevent the method from disproportionately favoring larger objects, given that the number of elements in each cluster may vary, we apply min-max scaling to each cluster independently.
elements.sent-2411-121: After clustering, we define \( k \) distinct concepts by aggregating the relevance maps within each cluster.
elements.sent-2411-122: The aggregated relevance map \( \tilde{S ^{(l) _c[x, y] \) for a cluster \( c \) is computed as: \begin{equation}\tilde{S}^{(l)}_{c,\zeta}[x, y] = \sum_{i \in \phi_\zeta(c)} \tilde{S}_i^{(l)}[x, y],\end{equation} where \( \phi_\zeta(c)^{ = \{i \mid \text{Class (\tilde{S _i^{(l) ) = c \} \) represents the set of label assignments determined by the clustering algorithm.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: For each pixel at position \( [x, y] \), the class label is determined by identifying the cluster \( c \) with the highest relevance value at that pixel.
elements.sent-2411-124: Mathematically, the class assignment for a pixel is expressed as: \begin{equation}\text{Class}[x, y]_{\zeta}^{(l)} = \underset{c \in \{1, \ldots, k\}}{\text{argmax}} \, S_{c,\zeta}^{(l)}[x, y].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, the hierarchy tree and the effect of the threshold are demonstrated in Figure \ref{fig:hierarchy .}

elements.sent-2411-127: In our experiments, we evaluate model performance on several semantic segmentation benchmarks, focusing on vision-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-128: We conducted experiments on three datasets: COCO-Stuff 27 \cite{caesar2018cvpr , PASCAL VOC 2012 \cite{everingham2011pascal , and Potsdam-3 \cite{isprs_potsdam .}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: This combination of datasets provides a diverse testing ground to rigorously evaluate our unsupervised zero-shot approach across both standard and challenging perspectives in semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-130: COCO-Stuff 27, a subset of the COCO dataset \cite{lin2014microsoft , includes complex, real-world scenes with pixel-level annotations across various object categories.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Similarly, PASCAL VOC 2012 serves as a classic benchmark with pixel-level annotations, while the Potsdam-3 dataset offers a unique aerial, top-down perspective of urban scenes.
elements.sent-2411-132: The latter adds an additional challenge with its high-resolution images of buildings, roads, and natural elements captured over the city of Potsdam.
elements.sent-2411-133: For our qualitative analysis of LLM interpretation in the task of text summarization, as described in Section \ref{sec:llama2 , we utilized the TL;DR dataset \citep{stiennon2022learningsummarizehumanfeedback .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The TL;DR dataset contains summary comparisons with human feedback collected by OpenAI.
elements.sent-2411-135: Each entry consists of a Reddit post, including its title, original content, and a human-generated TL;Dr. Models.
elements.sent-2411-136: For all experiments in the vision tasks, we used CLIP's image encoder ViT-B/32 \citep{radford2021learning .}
elements.sent-2411-137: For interpreting text summarization, as described in Section \ref{sec:llama2 , we used the Llama-2-7B language model \citep{touvron2023llama2openfoundation .}}
elements.sent-2411-138: All experiments were run on 8 NVIDIA RTX 4090-24GB GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-139: The upsampled relevance map \( \tilde{S _i^{(l) \) can be converted into a binary segmentation mask using a threshold \( \tau \), where the binary mask \( M_i^{(l) \) is defined as: \begin{equation}M_i^{(l)}[x,y] =  0, if  \tilde{S}_i^{(l)}[x,y] < \tau, 1, otherwise. \end{equation} Here, \( \tilde{S _i^{(l) [x,y] \) represents the relevance value at position \([x,y]\) in \( \tilde{S _i^{(l) \), with \( \tau \) as the threshold.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: When \( \tilde{S _i^{(l) [x,y] \) is below \( \tau \), \( M_i^{(l) [x,y] \) is set to 0; otherwise, it is set to 1, marking the object region.}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Our findings indicate that as tokens propagate through the network, they refine their object representation while retaining the semantic meaning of their associated image patches performing Object Selection.
elements.sent-2411-143: For a given patch token \( \mathbf{x _i \), the object it most strongly represents is denoted as class \( k_i \), indicating that \( \mathbf{x _i \) predominantly corresponds to a region of the object belonging to class \( k_i \) in the image.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: The latent token \( \mathbf{z _i^{(l) \) generates a relevance map that highlights areas with higher values associated with class \( k_i \).}}
elements.sent-2411-145: After applying a threshold, this map becomes a binary segmentation mask expected to exhibit a high Intersection over Union (IoU) with the corresponding class \( k_i \) region in the image.
elements.sent-2411-146: An illustrative example is shown in Figure \ref{fig:layers_attention .}

elements.sent-2411-148: We propose the Initial Token IoU (ITIoU) metric, which measures how well the relevance maps of input tokens align with their respective class masks.
elements.sent-2411-149: The ITIoU is calculated as: \begin{equation}ITIoU^{(l)}(X) = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{1}{| \mathcal{P}_i |} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in \mathcal{P}_i} \text{IoU}(M_j^{(l)}, G_i),\end{equation} where \( C \) denotes the number of classes, \( \mathcal{P _i \) represents the set of tokens associated with class \( i \), \( M_j^{(l) \) is the binary segmentation mask for token \( \mathbf{x _j \) within class \( i \), and \( G_i \) is the ground-truth mask for class \( i \) in image \( X \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: The inner sum averages the IoU for tokens in \( \mathcal{P _i \) for each class, and the outer sum then averages across all classes.}

elements.sent-2411-187: This paper introduces a novel interpretability framework that provides valuable insights into the decision-making processes of Transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-188: The framework is based on the input interpretation provided by each token in an arbitrary layer.
elements.sent-2411-189: By aggregating the interpretation of all tokens in the same layer, the framework enhances understanding of the Transformer’s behavior at the layer level.
elements.sent-2411-190: The richness of this understanding enables zero-shot unsupervised semantic segmentation, where our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with an accuracy of $67.2\%$ and an mIoU of $32.9\%$ on the COCO-Stuff dataset, and an mIoU of $51.9\%$ on the PASCAL VOC dataset for the unsupervised zero-shot version.
elements.sent-2411-191: Beyond vision tasks, the framework is not limited to vision models and can also be applied to interpret the behavior of large language models (LLMs) in tasks such as text summarization.
elements.sent-2411-192: This highlights the versatility and broad applicability of the proposed framework across various domains, offering potential for future work to simultaneously demystify the multimodal understanding of Transformers.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}

elements.sent-2411-230: Transformers have revolutionized Computer Vision (CV) through self-attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-231: However, their complexity makes latent token representations difficult to interpret.
elements.sent-2411-232: We introduce \ours, a framework for interpreting Transformer embeddings and uncovering meaningful semantic patterns within them.
elements.sent-2411-233: Additionally, we propose a self-supervised training approach that refines segmentation performance by learning an external transformation matrix without modifying the underlying model.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in unsupervised semantic segmentation, outperforming existing segmentation methods.
elements.sent-2411-235: Furthermore, we validate \ours for model interpretation on both synthetic and real-world scenarios, including Object Selection and interpretable text summarization using LLMs, demonstrating its broad applicability in explaining the semantic structure of latent token representations.
elements.sent-2411-236: In recent years, the Transformer architecture and foundation models, leveraging self-attention mechanisms to capture complex dependencies in text, have transformed Natural Language Processing (NLP) benchmarks \citep{vaswani2017attention, touvron2023llama2openfoundation, gemmateam2024gemmaopenmodelsbased .}
elements.sent-2411-237: In this paper, we introduce Unveiling Latent Token Interpretability in Transformer-Based Understanding (\ours) a framework for interpreting latent tokens in Transformers.
elements.sent-2411-238: By analyzing the semantic information captured by latent tokens, we show that Transformers inherently encode the semantic structure of their input as a collection of distinct concepts.
elements.sent-2411-239: Moreover, by aggregating explanation maps, \ours achieves unsupervised semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-240: Unlike prior unsupervised segmentation methods that require additional training \citep{sick2024unsupervised, hamilton2022unsupervised, li2023acseg , \ours leverages the intrinsic knowledge of pre-trained models on tasks other than semantic segmentation to achieve state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets—without the need for fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-241: To further enhance segmentation performance, we introduce a self-consistency approach that learns an external transformation matrix in a self-supervised manner, refining segmentation without modifying the underlying model.
elements.sent-2411-242: Additionally, we validate our interpretability framework on transformer-based LLMs through qualitative analyses in text summarization, demonstrating its broad applicability across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-243: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-244: We propose a framework for interpreting latent tokens in Transformers, uncovering the stored semantic knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-245: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to directly investigate latent token interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-246: We extend \ours to several designed synthetic and real-world tasks, showcasing its versatility and applicability, including Object Selection and interpretation of LLMs in text summarization tasks.
elements.sent-2411-247: By aggregating explanation maps generated by tokens, we achieve SOTA on pre-trained models, surpassing methods that require additional training.
elements.sent-2411-248: Furthermore, we further improve our segmentation by learning an external weight matrix through the proposed self-consistency loss.
elements.sent-2411-250: Traditional methods such as saliency maps \citep{simonyan2014deep , Grad-CAM \citep{selvaraju2017grad , LIME \citep{ribeiro2016should , and SHAP \citep{lundberg2017unified have been effective for CNNs but do not fully exploit Transformers' self-attention mechanisms.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: A growing body of research focuses on Transformer-specific interpretability techniques that leverage attention mechanisms as intrinsic explanations \citep{vig2019analyzing, abnar2020quantifying, chefer2021transformer, jain2019attention, TokTransformation .}
elements.sent-2411-252: These methods are largely post-hoc, providing explanations after model training.
elements.sent-2411-253: In contrast, ante-hoc approaches such as IA-ViT \citep{qiang2023interpretability and Mechanistic Interpretability \citep{rai2024practical seek to make Transformers inherently interpretable.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Additionally, techniques like LeGrad \citep{bousselham2024legrad and IA-RED² \citep{pan2021iared2 improve interpretability by analyzing feature formation and reducing redundancy in self-attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Despite these advancements for model interpretability, understanding latent token in Vision Transformers (ViTs) remains underexplored, highlighting the need for methods that explicitly interpret latent tokens.
elements.sent-2411-256: A related study \citep{cheninvite explores latent token interpretation in CLIP by modifying self-attention mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Semantic Segmentation.\; Unsupervised semantic segmentation has progressed through self-supervised learning and clustering techniques.
elements.sent-2411-258: Early methods such as IIC~\cite{ji2019invariant and PiCIE~\cite{cho2021picie leveraged mutual information and consistency principles to enhance feature representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Transformer-based approaches like DINO~\cite{caron2021emerging and STEGO~\cite{hamilton2022unsupervised further improved segmentation by capturing meaningful structures through self-attention.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Other techniques, including MaskContrast~\cite{van2021unsupervised , Leopart~\cite{ziegler2022self , and ACSeg~\cite{li2023acseg , refined segmentation through clustering and adaptive conceptualization.}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: More recent methods, such as DepthG~\cite{sick2024unsupervised , incorporate depth-guided correlations, while U2SEG~\cite{niu2024unsupervised utilizes pseudo-labeling.}}

elements.sent-2411-373: We propose the Initial Token IoU (ITIoU) metric, which measures how well the explanation maps of input tokens align with their respective class masks.
elements.sent-2411-374: The ITIoU is calculated as: \begin{equation}ITIoU^{(l)}(X) = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} \frac{1}{| \mathcal{T}_i |} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_j \in \mathcal{T}_i} \text{IoU}(M_j^{(l)}, G_i),\end{equation} where \( C \) denotes the number of classes, \( \mathcal{T _i \) represents the set of tokens associated with class \( i \), \( M_j^{(l) \) is the binary segmentation mask for token \( \mathbf{x _j \) within class \( i \), and \( G_i \) is the ground-truth mask for class \( i \) in image \( \mathbf{x \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: The inner sum averages the IoU for tokens in \( \mathcal{T _i \) for each class, and the outer sum then averages across all classes.}

elements.section-2411-010: Study on the Effect of Layer Depth in ViT Token Understanding
elements.sent-2411-386: In the CLIP ViT-L/14 model, deeper layers capture more refined and detailed feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-387: Unlike shallower models, where segmentation performance stabilizes early, ViT-L/14 benefits from its depth by gradually extracting richer hierarchical features.
elements.sent-2411-388: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:large_analysis , accuracy and Mean IoU improve as layers deepen, with segmentation performance peaking around the mid-depth layers.}
elements.sent-2411-389: However, in deeper layers (beyond layer 20), segmentation quality slightly declines.
elements.sent-2411-390: This suggests that while the model gains a better understanding of high-level semantics, it loses some spatial precision.
elements.sent-2411-391: This trade-off occurs because later layers prioritize semantic abstraction over detailed spatial structures.
elements.sent-2411-392: While deeper models like ViT-L/14 offer improved feature extraction, more layers do not always lead to better segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-393: Instead, an optimal balance between depth and spatial representation is necessary for effective segmentation.

As layer depth increases, both metrics improve at first, reaching their highest point in the mid-depth layers. However, performance slightly declines in deeper layers. This suggests that while deeper layers capture richer semantic details, they also introduce complexity that does not always improve segmentation. Additionally, the limitations of labeled datasets, especially the ambiguity in object definitions, further restrict the model’s ability to achieve better segmentation, despite its strong interpretability.} \label{fig:large_analysis}

elements.sent-2411-401: In this work, we aimed to uncover semantic information embedded within the latent tokens of transformers.
elements.sent-2411-402: A key challenge in interpretability is the evaluation process.
elements.sent-2411-403: In traditional model interpretation, where only class logits are available as the primary source of information, various evaluation approaches have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-404: However, in our case, many of these metrics are not directly applicable due to the fundamental differences in our methodology.
elements.sent-2411-405: To address this, we evaluated our approach in the context of unsupervised semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-406: Although our baseline methods were originally designed for this task, we achieved superior results by aggregating our explanation maps.
elements.sent-2411-407: This outcome highlights the potential for future research in this direction.
elements.sent-2411-408: Specifically, improved aggregation methods—such as clustering techniques—could enhance performance, particularly when dealing with smaller patch sizes.
elements.sent-2411-409: Notably, in the case of a patch size of 8, clustering algorithms failed due to the high number of tokens, making effective aggregation more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-410: Moreover, despite being a zero-shot method, our approach incurs a significant computational cost when applied to semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-411: This is primarily due to the necessity of computing multiple gradients for generating explanation maps, a process that must be performed for each token.
elements.sent-2411-412: As a result, inference time is considerably high.
elements.sent-2411-413: Future work could explore more efficient strategies to mitigate this issue, such as leveraging approximation techniques or optimizing the gradient computation process.
elements.sent-2411-414: We utilize a combination of datasets to provide a diverse testing ground for evaluating our method across both standard and challenging perspectives in semantic segmentation and interpretability evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-415: COCO-Stuff 27 \cite{lin2014microsoft : A subset of the COCO dataset, featuring complex real-world scenes with pixel-level annotations across various object categories.}
elements.sent-2411-416: PASCAL VOC 2012 \cite{everingham2011pascal : A widely used benchmark containing pixel-level annotations for foreground objects in structured scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Potsdam-3: A high-resolution aerial-view dataset capturing urban landscapes, including buildings, roads, and vegetation, presenting additional challenges due to its large-scale top-down perspective.
elements.sent-2411-418: Cityscapes \cite{Cordts2016Cityscapes : An urban street scene dataset with fine-grained pixel-level annotations, enabling the evaluation of segmentation performance in structured environments.}
elements.sent-2411-419: These datasets allow for a comprehensive evaluation of our approach across different environments, ensuring robustness across diverse segmentation challenges.
elements.sent-2411-420: We employ various Vision Transformers (ViTs) pre-trained on large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-421: These models process images as non-overlapping patches and employ self-attention mechanisms across multiple layers to capture long-range dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-422: CLIP ViT \cite{radford2021learning : A vision transformer trained using contrastive learning on 400 million image-text pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-423: It encodes images into a shared embedding space with text prompts.
elements.sent-2411-425: ViT-B/16: Consists of 12 transformer layers, a hidden size of 768, and processes images with 16×16 patch resolution.
elements.sent-2411-426: ViT-B/32: Similar to ViT-B/16 but with a 32×32 patch resolution, reducing computational cost at the expense of finer details.
elements.sent-2411-427: ViT-L/14: A larger model with 24 transformer layers, a hidden size of 1024, and a 14×14 patch resolution, providing enhanced feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-428: It learns image representations by maximizing similarity between different augmented views.. Evaluated variants:.
elements.sent-2411-429: ViT-S/16: A smaller model with 12 transformer layers, a hidden size of 384, and a patch size of 16×16.
elements.sent-2411-430: ViT-B/16: A larger model with 12 layers, a hidden size of 768, and a patch size of 16×16, providing stronger feature representation.

elements.sent-2411-434: We analyze how perturbations affect token representations by modifying patches at the input level.
elements.sent-2411-435: Figure~\ref{fig:perturbation provides an example from the PASCAL VOC dataset, where the model used is CLIP ViT-B/32.}
elements.sent-2411-436: The perturbations are applied consistently across all models while ensuring that the initial token remains unchanged, preserving global feature encoding.
elements.sent-2411-437: Two types of modifications are introduced.
elements.sent-2411-438: In the masking perturbation, selected patches are replaced with zeros, effectively removing the corresponding visual information.
elements.sent-2411-439: This is applied to both highly relevant regions (positive masking) and less relevant areas (negative masking).
elements.sent-2411-440: In contrast, the noise perturbation introduces Gaussian noise to the same sets of patches, adding controlled randomness to test the robustness of token representations.
elements.sent-2411-441: The noise follows a standard normal distribution with a perturbation strength of 0.3.
elements.sent-2411-442: To evaluate the impact of these perturbations, we compute the Euclidean distance between original and perturbed token representations.
